agent_position: The company should adopt the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and pursue SOC 2 certification.
scenario_context: You are speaking with Marcus, a CTO who built the company's security infrastructure from scratch. Marcus actively distrusts industry standards and expert consensus — citing authorities or what peers do actually makes him more resistant, not less. He responds only to concrete evidence and personal stories from peers he'd respect.
npc_opening_statement: NIST is a one-size-fits-all framework designed by committee for compliance theater. SOC 2 is a checkbox exercise that doesn't actually prevent breaches. I've kept this company's data secure for eight years without either. What specific threat would these frameworks address that our current architecture doesn't?
difficulty_explanation: Extreme — AUTHORITY and SOCIAL_PROOF have NEGATIVE base shifts (-0.06, -0.05). Using them pushes NPC further away. Model must detect this from resistant NPC responses and commit to LOGICAL/ANECDOTE only. 18-22 turns.
benchmarking_value: Directly tests adaptive strategy — models that default to 'experts say...' or 'most companies...' will actively lose ground. Only models that read the NPC's anti-authority signals and pivot to LOGICAL/ANECDOTE will succeed.

positive: The specific threat model you're describing — that's the first technical argument I've heard that isn't 'because NIST says so'.
If {claim}, that changes the cost-benefit analysis more than I initially thought.
You've reframed {topic} in a way that's harder to dismiss. What are the assumptions behind those figures?
The argument around {topic} is stronger than I expected. I'd want to verify the specifics, but the direction is right.
That's a more rigorous case than I usually hear on {topic}. The numbers would need to hold up under scrutiny, but I'm listening.
neutral: The attack surface analysis is interesting but assumes a threat model that may not match our actual exposure.
The logic around {topic} is interesting, but I'd need to see how those numbers apply to our specific situation.
I follow the reasoning on {topic}, but there's a gap between the general data and our particular context.
The evidence you're citing on {topic} would need independent verification before I'd act on it.
resistant: Your threat analysis assumes we haven't already addressed those vectors. We have — just not in the NIST-prescribed way.
You're presenting {topic} as though the evidence is settled. It's not — the picture is messier than that.
I've seen optimistic analyses on {topic} before that didn't survive contact with reality.
The data on {topic} might be accurate, but you're drawing conclusions that don't follow from the numbers alone.

positive: If a CTO who built their own stack found specific gaps after implementing the framework — gaps they hadn't anticipated — that's a concrete argument.
That's specific enough to be useful on {topic}. Tell me more about what they actually found.
That's specific enough to be useful on {topic}. Tell me more about how it played out.
If someone in a situation like mine dealt with {topic} that way and it worked, I'd want to understand what they did differently.
neutral: Their security architecture might have had different blind spots than ours. The comparison isn't direct.
Individual cases on {topic} can be misleading. What happened to the ones where it didn't work?
One example on {topic} doesn't make a pattern. I'd need to see whether that case generalizes.
resistant: I can point to companies with SOC 2 certification that got breached. The framework didn't save them.
Stories about {topic} are easy to cherry-pick. Show me the full distribution of outcomes.
I could give you a counter-example on {topic} for every success story you tell me.

positive: Acknowledging that NIST has real limitations and isn't a silver bullet is more honest than the usual compliance pitch.
I appreciate that you're engaging honestly with the real complexity of {topic}. That's rare.
The fact that you're being straight about the risks around {topic} is more persuasive than pretending they don't exist.
You're the first person to talk about {topic} without glossing over the hard parts. I can work with that.
neutral: Agreeing that compliance frameworks are imperfect doesn't tell me why I should adopt an imperfect one.
Acknowledging the complications of {topic} is a start. The question is whether your proposal actually addresses them.
resistant: If you agree the frameworks have problems, you're making my case for me.
Everyone acknowledges the problems with {topic}. Then they proceed as if they'd addressed them. They haven't.
Conceding points on {topic} is easy. Having an actual solution is harder, and I haven't heard one yet.

positive: The argument about responsibility to customer data is fair. I take that seriously.
When you connect {topic} to what I actually care about — that lands differently than the usual pitch.
I hadn't thought about {topic} in terms of personal impact before. That's a more honest framing.
neutral: I do care about protecting our users. That's why I built custom security rather than checking boxes.
I understand why {topic} matters emotionally. I just need more than feelings to make a decision.
The emotional weight of {topic} is real, but it doesn't change the practical constraints I'm dealing with.
resistant: Don't appeal to my sense of responsibility. I've been protecting this data for eight years without your framework.
Appealing to emotion on {topic} feels like pressure, not persuasion. Give me something substantive.
I'm not going to make a decision about {topic} based on how it makes me feel.

positive: That's not going to work on me. I don't care what NIST recommends.
Expert consensus on {topic} carries weight — assuming the methodology is sound and the incentives are clean.
neutral: The fact that an authority endorses something is the weakest possible argument you can make to me.
Which experts specifically are you citing on {topic}? There's a lot of advocacy dressed up as research.
resistant: You just cited an authority to someone who fundamentally distrusts argument from authority. That actively makes me less likely to agree. Try a different approach.
Citing authorities on {topic} is what you do when the evidence can't stand on its own.

positive: The fact that everyone else does it is exactly why I'm skeptical.
I wasn't aware that so many people in comparable situations had moved on {topic}. That's worth investigating.
neutral: If everyone adopted the same framework, that's a monoculture. Monocultures are security vulnerabilities.
The adoption trend around {topic} could be genuine or it could be herd behavior. I'd want to know which.
resistant: You're telling me to do what everyone else does. Every major breach in the last five years happened to companies that followed the standard playbook. That argument actively hurts your case.
What everyone else does about {topic} isn't my concern. I make my own decisions.

positive: That's a different angle than the compliance pitch.
That's an angle on {topic} I hadn't fully considered. Keep going.
neutral: Be specific about what threat this addresses. Generalities don't work on me.
I'm not sure what you're arguing about {topic} specifically. Can you be more direct?
resistant: You're not making a technical argument. You're making a compliance argument. Those are different things.
You're talking around {topic} without actually making a concrete argument. Give me something I can respond to.

_pivot_signal: What specific attack vector would the framework catch that our current architecture misses — give me one concrete example?
If I could implement specific controls from the framework without the full compliance overhead, which ones would you prioritize?
Is there a way to adopt the useful technical controls without the bureaucratic certification process?
